lesson.menu_desc: Reading the room across business cultures: interpret signals with modals of deduction (might, must, can't), adapt your directness, and protect the relationship — 33 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 9 — Cross-Cultural Communication — Reading the Room Across Business Cultures
lesson.title_tag: Professor View — Victor Malvezi Paschotto | Aula 9 — Cross-Cultural Communication

listenings.text: Okay, before this call, let me read the situation. I have three offices on the line: our partners in Tokyo, our supplier in Frankfurt, and Elena's team in Buenos Aires. They do not all communicate the same way, so I cannot use one single style. The Frankfurt team values directness, so they might find a long warm-up strange; with them, I should get to the point quickly. The Tokyo office is more careful, so a quiet pause from them does not mean a no. It might mean they are still building consensus, and I must not misread that silence. In Buenos Aires, the relationship comes first, so a few minutes of small talk will build rapport before we touch the numbers. My job today is not to be the loudest voice. It is to read the room, adapt my tone to each side, and make sure nobody has to lose face. If I get the etiquette right, the content will land. So let me listen more than I speak.

Hi Victor, it is Nadia. Before your cross-cultural call, three quick reminders. First, treat every cultural pattern as a tendency, not a rule. People are individuals, so read the person in front of you, not the stereotype. A norm tells you what might be happening; it never tells you for certain. Second, when a signal confuses you, do not guess in silence. Check it. Say, I want to make sure I understand you correctly, and let them clarify. That one habit prevents most misunderstandings before they grow. Third, protect the relationship over the point. If someone is wrong, give them a way to save face, and never correct a senior colleague in front of their team. And watch your own directness. What feels honest to you can come across as blunt to someone else, so soften the edges and add a little warmth. Read the room, adapt, and stay curious. You are good at this, Victor. Go build the bridge.
